items.title: McDonald Criteria 2017
items.contentHtml:
McDonald Criteria 2017

  • Dissemination in space (DIS): ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 of 4 regions: periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord.
  • Dissemination in time (DIT): simultaneous presence of enhancing (active) and non-enhancing (inactive) lesions on any magnetic resonance imaging; or new T2 lesion on follow-up magnetic resonance imaging.
  • cerebrospinal fluid with oligoclonal bands (OCB) can replace DIT if DIS and typical symptoms are met.
  • Trap: the diagnosis requires ruling out other diseases (NMO, sarcoidosis).

items.title: Clinical phenotypes
items.contentHtml:
Clinical forms

  • Relapsing-remitting (RRMS): acute flares with complete or partial recovery; no progression between outbreaks. It represents 85% at the beginning.
  • Primary progressive (PPMS): neurological deterioration from the beginning, without defined outbreaks; more common in men and >40 years old.
  • Secondary progressive (SPMS): evolves from RRMS with gradual progression, with or without superimposed flares.
  • Clinically isolated (CIS): first demyelinating episode; If it meets DIS and DIT it is classified as RRMS.

items.foco: Periventricular demyelinating lesions on magnetic resonance imaging
items.title: magnetic resonance imaging injuries
items.contentHtml:
Findings on brain magnetic resonance imaging

  • Periventricular lesions: hyperintense on T2/FLAIR, typically oval and perpendicular to the ventricular axis (Dawson's fingers).
  • Enhancement after gadolinium: nodular or annular uptake (incomplete) indicates acute inflammatory activity.
  • Key locations: corpus callosum (lesions) at the callosal-septal interface), spinal cord (short, eccentric, <2 vertebral segments).
  • Trap: in NMO the spinal lesions are longitudinally extensive (>3 segments) and the enhancement may be more homogeneous.

items.foco: Oligoclonal bands in cerebrospinal fluid as a marker
items.title: Oligoclonal bands
items.contentHtml:
Oligoclonal bands in cerebrospinal fluid

  • Definition: IgG bands detected by isoelectric focusing in cerebrospinal fluid absent in serum (type 2 pattern).
  • Sensitivity: >90% in confirmed MS; They may be transient in other diseases (infections, NMO).
  • IgG index: (cerebrospinal fluid IgG/cerebrospinal fluid albumin)/(serum IgG/serum albumin); >0.7 suggests intrathecal synthesis.
  • Trap: the absence of BOC does not rule out MS, but forces us to reconsider differential diagnosis.

items.title: Optic neuritis
items.contentHtml:
Optic neuritis in MS

  • Presentation: ocular pain with movements, unilateral subacute visual loss, relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn).
  • Recovery: spontaneous in weeks; methylprednisolone 1 g/day intravenous for 3-5 days accelerates recovery without improving final prognosis.
  • Findings: optic nerve edema on magnetic resonance imaging with enhancement; visual evoked potentials with long latency.
  • Trap: if there is simultaneous bilateral visual loss, consider NMO (anti-AQP4 antibodies).

items.title: Uhthoff phenomenon
items.contentHtml:
Uhthoff phenomenon

  • Definition: transient worsening of neurological symptoms (blurred vision, fatigue) due to increased body temperature (exercise, fever, hot bath).
  • Mechanism: nerve conduction block in demyelinated axons due to increased temperature, without new inflammation.
  • Pseudooutbreak: similar symptom to flare but without evidence of active lesion on magnetic resonance imaging or enhancement with gadolinium.
  • Trap: Differentiate from true flare; The pseudo-outbreak reverses upon cooling and does not require steroid treatment.

items.foco: Treatment of acute flare with methylprednisolone
items.title: Acute outbreak treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of acute outbreak

  • First line: methylprednisolone 1 g/day intravenous for 3-5 days; equivalent to high-dose oral dexamethasone or prednisone.
  • Indication: flare with significant functional impairment (vision, gait, strength).
  • Mechanism: reduces edema and inflammation; accelerates recovery but does not modify long-term disability.
  • If no response: consider plasmapheresis (5-7 sessions) in severe corticorefractory flares.
  • Trap: do not use oral corticosteroids at low doses; do not prolong >5 days without evidence.

items.title: Modifying therapies
items.contentHtml:
Disease-modifying therapies

  • Interferon beta (1a/1b): first line in RRMS; reduces outbreak rate 30%; adverse effects: flu-like syndrome, elevation of transaminases.
  • Fingolimod: S1P receptor modulator; sequesters lymphocytes in lymph nodes; requires cardiac (initial bradycardia) and ocular (macular edema) monitoring.
  • Natalizumab: anti-α4-integrin antibody; high efficiency; risk of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) associated with positive JCV.
  • Catch: natalizumab is reserved for active RRMS; Before starting, test anti-JCV antibodies.

items.foco: Lhermitte sign as an early manifestation
items.title: Lhermitte sign
items.contentHtml:
Lhermitte sign

  • Definition: sensation of electric shock that runs through the spine when flexing the neck (cervical flexion maneuver).
  • Mechanism: irritation of demyelinated axons in the cervical spinal cord, typically in posterior cords.
  • Association: early sign of MS; It can also occur in spinal cord compression, myelitis, B12 deficiency.
  • Trap: it is not pathognomonic of MS; requires cervical magnetic resonance imaging to rule out compressive lesions.

items.foco: Dysarthria, ataxia and tremor in advanced forms
items.title: Symptoms in advanced MS
items.contentHtml:
Dysarthria, ataxia and tremor

  • Cerebellar dysarthria: scanned speech (slow, staccato) due to injury to the cerebellar pathways.
  • Ataxia: incoordination of limbs and gait; It can be trunk or appendicular.
  • Tremor: intentional (kinetic) and postural tremor; frequently refractory to treatment.
  • Management: physiotherapy, beta blockers (propranolol), clonazepam; in severe cases, deep brain stimulation (thalamus).
  • Trap: these symptoms usually appear in progressive MS and are associated with irreversible disability.

items.title: Differential diagnosis: NMO vs EM
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis NMO

  • NMO: anti-AQP4 antibodies (aquaporin 4) positive in 70-80%; attacks astrocytes, not myelin.
  • Key differences: severe bilateral optic neuritis, longitudinally extensive myelitis (>3 segments), atypical brain lesions (periaqueductal, diencephalic).
  • cerebrospinal fluid: oligoclonal bands are rarely present in NMO; there may be neutrophilic pleocytosis.
  • Treatment: acute flare with methylprednisolone + plasmapheresis; maintenance with mycophenolate, rituximab, or eculizumab; Interferon and fingolimod may worsen NMO.

items.foco: EDSS scale to evaluate neurological disability
items.title: EDSS scale
items.contentHtml:
EDSS Scale (Kurtzke)

  • Range: 0 (normal examination) to 10 (death due to MS).
  • Key points: 0-4.5: patient ambulates without assistance; 5.0-6.5: requires cane or walker; 7.0: confined to a wheelchair.
  • Based on: evaluation of 8 functional systems (pyramidal, cerebellar, trunk, sensory, visual, sphincters, cerebral, others).
  • Trap: changes of 0.5 points can be clinically significant; It is not linear (disability progresses faster with lower scores).
